ideas.title: Upgrade storm drain capacity at Elm Street underpass
ideas.body: Commission a hydraulic engineering assessment and upgrade the storm drain at the Elm Street underpass. Reroute surface water runoff from surrounding streets into an enlarged drain. Estimated cost: £40,000 from the highways maintenance fund.

amendments.proposedTitle: Upgrade Elm Street underpass: drain, lighting, and cycle access
amendments.proposedBody: Commission a hydraulic engineering assessment and upgrade the storm drain at the Elm Street underpass. Reroute surface water runoff from surrounding streets into an enlarged drain. Estimated cost: £40,000 from the highways maintenance fund. Extend the scope to include improved lighting, tactile paving, and a separated one-metre cycle lane through the underpass.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Add pedestrian and cycle safety improvements
amendments.amendmentDescription: The underpass is narrow and poorly lit regardless of flooding. A coordinated project is more cost-effective and is more likely to attract active travel funding that the drain upgrade alone would not qualify for.

supporters.reason: Cycling through there is genuinely dangerous. Combining the projects makes sense.
Active travel funding could cover a significant portion of the additional cost.

opponents.reason: Expanding scope risks delaying the drain fix, which is the urgent problem.

ideas.title: Community garden on Pine Avenue vacant lot
ideas.body: Work with the council to convert the vacant lot at 14 Pine Avenue into allotment plots. Establish a resident committee to manage applications, the waiting list, and shared maintenance. Target: operational within 12 months.

amendments.proposedTitle: Community garden with tool library and composting bays
amendments.proposedBody: Work with the council to convert the vacant lot at 14 Pine Avenue into allotment plots. Establish a resident committee to manage applications, the waiting list, and shared maintenance. Target: operational within 12 months. Include a shared tool store (stocked via donation drive) and at least two composting bays serving the wider street.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Add shared tool library and composting bays
amendments.amendmentDescription: A tool store removes the main cost barrier for new plot holders. The composting bays would serve the whole street and reduce organic waste going to landfill.

supporters.reason: Tools are the main cost barrier for new growers. A shared store makes this accessible.
Composting bays would be useful for residents well beyond the garden itself.
Donation drives work well for tools. Low cost, high community involvement.

amendments.proposedTitle: Community garden with priority allocation for households without garden access
amendments.proposedBody: Work with the council to convert the vacant lot at 14 Pine Avenue into allotment plots. Establish a resident committee to manage applications, the waiting list, and shared maintenance. Target: operational within 12 months. Reserve at least half of all plots, by ballot, for households with no private outdoor growing space.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Reserve plots for households without garden access
amendments.amendmentDescription: Without an allocation policy the plots will go to whoever applies first, which tends to favour those already well-resourced. A reserved allocation ensures the garden serves residents with the greatest need.

supporters.reason: Flat allocation means residents with gardens get allotments while flat dwellers miss out.
A ballot is fair and transparent.

opponents.reason: Verifying 'no garden' status could be burdensome and contentious to administer.

ideas.title: Replace failing Maple Road lamp columns with LED fittings
ideas.body: Replace the seven failing sodium lamp columns on Maple Road with full-spectrum LED fittings on a managed maintenance contract. Extend the lighting zone 80 metres north to cover the school gate approach. Funding to be drawn from the highways capital improvement budget. Target completion before the autumn term.

amendments.proposedTitle: Replace Maple Road lamp columns and introduce timed dimming to reduce light pollution
amendments.proposedBody: Replace the seven failing sodium lamp columns on Maple Road with full-spectrum LED fittings on a managed maintenance contract. Extend the lighting zone 80 metres north to cover the school gate approach. Funding to be drawn from the highways capital improvement budget. Target completion before the autumn term. Configure all new fittings with timed dimming between midnight and 5 am to reduce light pollution and energy costs.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Add timed dimming to reduce light pollution and energy use
amendments.amendmentDescription: Modern LED columns support dimming schedules at no additional hardware cost. Night-time dimming reduces ecological light pollution and lowers the running cost, improving the long-term case for the upgrade.

supporters.reason: The ecological argument is sound. Full brightness at 3 am serves almost no one.
Lower energy costs make this an easier sell to the council finance team.

opponents.reason: Any dimming scheme must still guarantee visibility outside the school. The timings need careful design.

ideas.title: Commission a full accessibility audit and phased resurfacing of Riverside Park paths
ideas.body: Commission an independent accessibility audit of all Riverside Park footpaths to identify and prioritise sections that exclude wheelchair users and pushchair users. Use audit findings to produce a phased resurfacing plan, with the northern loop section completed first. Apply for Sport England Inclusive Activity funding to supplement the parks maintenance budget.

amendments.proposedTitle: Riverside Park accessibility audit, resurfacing, and new rest seating
amendments.proposedBody: Commission an independent accessibility audit of all Riverside Park footpaths to identify and prioritise sections that exclude wheelchair users and pushchair users. Use audit findings to produce a phased resurfacing plan, with the northern loop section completed first. Apply for Sport England Inclusive Activity funding to supplement the parks maintenance budget. Include installation of six additional benches at regular intervals along the main loop, reducing the distance between rest points to no more than 100 metres.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Include rest seating at regular intervals
amendments.amendmentDescription: Older residents and those with limited mobility are excluded not only by surface quality but by the absence of places to rest. Additional seating is low cost and dramatically increases how far many people can comfortably walk.

supporters.reason: My father uses the park but cannot complete the loop without somewhere to sit. Seating would transform his experience.
Seating is cheap relative to resurfacing but high impact for older users.
This should have been part of the original park design. Fixing it now is overdue.

amendments.proposedTitle: Riverside Park accessibility audit with co-design sessions led by disabled residents
amendments.proposedBody: Commission an independent accessibility audit of all Riverside Park footpaths to identify and prioritise sections that exclude wheelchair users and pushchair users. Use audit findings to produce a phased resurfacing plan, with the northern loop section completed first. Apply for Sport England Inclusive Activity funding to supplement the parks maintenance budget. Convene at least two co-design sessions with disabled residents and pushchair users before finalising the resurfacing specification, to ensure lived-experience priorities are reflected.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Require co-design with disabled residents before finalising specification
amendments.amendmentDescription: Technical audits identify deficiencies but do not capture the routes and features that disabled residents actually want. Co-design sessions ensure money is spent on changes that matter most to the people affected.

supporters.reason: As a wheelchair user I would rather be consulted than have someone guess what I need.
Co-design produces better outcomes and increases community ownership of the result.

opponents.reason: Co-design adds time. The northern section is already impassable and needs fixing now, not after two consultation rounds.

ideas.title: Establish a community-run meanwhile space in the Cedar Lane Youth Centre building
ideas.body: Negotiate a meanwhile licence with the council for the Cedar Lane Youth Centre building. Form a volunteer steering group to operate a weekly programme of evening activities for 11–18 year olds while a longer-term funding solution is sought. Partner with local sports clubs and arts organisations to provide activities at low or no cost.

amendments.proposedTitle: Community meanwhile space in Cedar Lane Youth Centre with youth-led programme committee
amendments.proposedBody: Negotiate a meanwhile licence with the council for the Cedar Lane Youth Centre building. Form a volunteer steering group to operate a weekly programme of evening activities for 11–18 year olds while a longer-term funding solution is sought. Partner with local sports clubs and arts organisations to provide activities at low or no cost. Establish a programme committee where at least half the seats are held by young people aged 14–18, with meaningful decision-making authority over what activities run.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Give young people majority control of the programme committee
amendments.amendmentDescription: Adult-designed youth provision often misses what young people actually want. A programme committee with genuine youth majority ensures activities are relevant and increases attendance and ownership.

supporters.reason: Every youth programme I have seen that was designed without young people struggled with attendance.
Young people knowing they have real authority is itself a valuable experience.
This is straightforward to implement and makes the whole project more credible.

amendments.proposedTitle: Community meanwhile space in Cedar Lane with parallel application for statutory youth funding
amendments.proposedBody: Negotiate a meanwhile licence with the council for the Cedar Lane Youth Centre building. Form a volunteer steering group to operate a weekly programme of evening activities for 11–18 year olds while a longer-term funding solution is sought. Partner with local sports clubs and arts organisations to provide activities at low or no cost. Simultaneously submit a formal application under the council's Section 106 youth provision obligation to restore statutory funding for the facility.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Pursue statutory Section 106 funding in parallel
amendments.amendmentDescription: The council has planning contributions earmarked for youth provision under Section 106 agreements. The meanwhile space addresses the immediate gap, but a formal funding application pursues the permanent solution in parallel rather than treating the temporary arrangement as an end state.

supporters.reason: Voluntary effort should not become a substitute for statutory provision. We need both tracks running.
The Section 106 argument is strong. The council has obligations here.

opponents.reason: A funding application will take months. The meanwhile space should open immediately without waiting.

ideas.title: Meanwhile use programme for vacant High Street units
ideas.body: Work with the council's economic development team and the Business Improvement District to offer a meanwhile use licence to local makers, artists, community groups, and social enterprises for High Street units that have been vacant for more than twelve months. Target a minimum of five units activated within six months. Active frontages improve the experience for remaining businesses and demonstrate to landlords that occupancy is achievable.

amendments.proposedTitle: Meanwhile use programme with priority for community and social enterprise occupiers
amendments.proposedBody: Work with the council's economic development team and the Business Improvement District to offer a meanwhile use licence to local makers, artists, community groups, and social enterprises for High Street units that have been vacant for more than twelve months. Target a minimum of five units activated within six months. Active frontages improve the experience for remaining businesses and demonstrate to landlords that occupancy is achievable. Reserve at least three of the five initial units for non-commercial community or social enterprise use, to ensure the programme serves public benefit and not only commercial revival.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Reserve majority of units for community and social enterprise use
amendments.amendmentDescription: Without explicit allocation, meanwhile use programmes are quickly dominated by commercial pop-ups with low community benefit. Reserving units for non-commercial occupiers creates spaces that residents actually use and generates broader support for the programme.

supporters.reason: Commercial pop-ups are fine but they serve customers. Community spaces serve residents. We need both.
A repair café or food bank in a High Street unit would do more for the community than another market stall.
Non-commercial use is also easier to get landlord agreement on — less risk of tenancy disputes.

opponents.reason: The programme needs to be attractive to a wide range of occupiers. Rigid quotas may reduce take-up.

ideas.title: Expand the community air quality sensor network and require council response protocol
ideas.body: Extend the existing community sensor network around the Northfield Industrial Estate from six to twenty sensors to provide full coverage of the affected residential area. Publish real-time data on an open dashboard. Submit formal evidence to the council's Environmental Health team under the Environment Act and request a written response within 28 days committing to an investigation timeline.

amendments.proposedTitle: Expand sensor network and commission independent health impact assessment
amendments.proposedBody: Extend the existing community sensor network around the Northfield Industrial Estate from six to twenty sensors to provide full coverage of the affected residential area. Publish real-time data on an open dashboard. Submit formal evidence to the council's Environmental Health team under the Environment Act and request a written response within 28 days committing to an investigation timeline. Commission an independent health impact assessment of the estate's emissions, drawing on GP respiratory referral data where consent allows, to build an evidence base that cannot be dismissed.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Commission independent health impact assessment using NHS data
amendments.amendmentDescription: Sensor data alone can be contested by the operators. A health impact assessment linking emissions to recorded respiratory outcomes creates a much stronger case for regulatory action and demonstrates the human cost rather than just the technical exceedance.

supporters.reason: My daughter's asthma records are exactly the kind of evidence this assessment would use.
Operators will challenge sensor methodology. Health data is much harder to dismiss.
This also creates a baseline for monitoring whether conditions improve after any intervention.

opponents.reason: A health impact assessment takes time and money. The sensor evidence is already clear enough to demand action now.

ideas.title: Install a signalised pedestrian crossing on School Road
ideas.body: Apply to the council's highways team for a signalised crossing on School Road at the midpoint between Park Lane junction and the school gate, approximately 175 metres from each end. Present the documented near-miss incidents as grounds for a formal road safety assessment. Request that the assessment be completed and a decision issued within three months.

amendments.proposedTitle: Signalised crossing on School Road with 20 mph zone and pavement widening
amendments.proposedBody: Apply to the council's highways team for a signalised crossing on School Road at the midpoint between Park Lane junction and the school gate, approximately 175 metres from each end. Present the documented near-miss incidents as grounds for a formal road safety assessment. Request that the assessment be completed and a decision issued within three months. Extend the scope of the application to include a 20 mph zone covering the full length of School Road and widening of the pavement on the school-side footway to allow two pushchairs to pass.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Add 20 mph zone and pavement widening to scope
amendments.amendmentDescription: A single crossing addresses one point but not the broader danger. A 20 mph zone reduces vehicle speed for the whole route and is proven to reduce both the frequency and severity of collisions. Pavement widening removes a separate hazard where parents and children must step into the road to pass each other.

supporters.reason: The crossing is the minimum. 20 mph and a wider pavement make the whole street safe.
20 mph zones are consistently effective and cost far less than collision response.
Schools should be in low-speed zones as a default, not an exception.

opponents.reason: Expanding scope risks the whole application being deferred for a wider traffic study. A crossing is achievable now.

amendments.proposedTitle: Signalised crossing on School Road with School Streets restriction at peak times
amendments.proposedBody: Apply to the council's highways team for a signalised crossing on School Road at the midpoint between Park Lane junction and the school gate, approximately 175 metres from each end. Present the documented near-miss incidents as grounds for a formal road safety assessment. Request that the assessment be completed and a decision issued within three months. In parallel, pilot a School Streets restriction closing School Road to through-traffic between 8–9 am and 3–4 pm on school days, using volunteer marshals initially and Traffic Regulation Orders if the pilot succeeds.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Pilot a School Streets restriction to close the road at pick-up and drop-off
amendments.amendmentDescription: The danger is concentrated at peak school times. Closing the road to through-traffic during those windows removes the hazard entirely rather than managing it. School Streets pilots are low cost, reversible, and have strong track records in other areas.

supporters.reason: School Streets schemes are proven. The risk is low and the benefit could be transformative.
A pilot is inherently reversible. If it does not work, it costs little.

opponents.reason: Closing the road will push traffic onto residential side streets. We need to model the displacement before committing.

ideas.title: Restore Central Library to seven-day opening through a community trust model
ideas.body: Establish a community trust to take on operational management of the Central Library under a peppercorn lease from the council, enabling the building to remain open seven days a week. The trust model removes the facility from the direct council budget while keeping it in community ownership. Seek grant funding from Arts Council England and the Carnegie UK Trust for the transition and first three years of operation.

amendments.proposedTitle: Community trust library with warm space, digital access hub, and peer support services
amendments.proposedBody: Establish a community trust to take on operational management of the Central Library under a peppercorn lease from the council, enabling the building to remain open seven days a week. The trust model removes the facility from the direct council budget while keeping it in community ownership. Seek grant funding from Arts Council England and the Carnegie UK Trust for the transition and first three years of operation. Formalise the library's role as a warm space and digital access hub, and co-locate a peer support drop-in service for adults experiencing social isolation.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Formalise warm space, digital access, and peer support co-location
amendments.amendmentDescription: The library already serves these functions informally. Naming them explicitly in the trust's remit unlocks specific funding streams (DCMS Warm Spaces, National Lottery Community Fund, NHS social prescribing budgets) that the library alone would not qualify for.

supporters.reason: The library is already a de facto warm space. Making it official brings funding.
Digital access and job search support could be formalised alongside this at almost no extra cost.
Social prescribing referrals from the GP surgery two streets away would be a natural fit.

amendments.proposedTitle: Restore library hours through community trust while pursuing judicial review of consultation failure
amendments.proposedBody: Establish a community trust to take on operational management of the Central Library under a peppercorn lease from the council, enabling the building to remain open seven days a week. The trust model removes the facility from the direct council budget while keeping it in community ownership. Seek grant funding from Arts Council England and the Carnegie UK Trust for the transition and first three years of operation. In parallel, commission a legal opinion on whether the council's failure to conduct a public consultation before cutting library hours breached its statutory obligations, with a view to a judicial review application if the opinion is favourable.
amendments.amendmentTitle: Pursue legal challenge to the consultation failure in parallel
amendments.amendmentDescription: The council cut hours without any public consultation. That may constitute an unlawful failure of due process. A judicial review would not reverse the cuts immediately but would establish accountability and set a precedent that prevents the same approach being used to cut other services.

supporters.reason: The community trust is the practical solution. The legal route is about accountability. Both are necessary.
If the council did breach its duties, that should be established on the record.

opponents.reason: A legal challenge could antagonise the council at exactly the moment we need their cooperation on the trust lease.
